section: Low-density $\mathrm { F e _ { 4 0 } M n _ { 1 9 } N i _ { 1 5 } A l _ { 1 5 } S i _ { 1 0 } C _ { 1 } }$ high entropy steel processed by mechanical alloying and spark plasma sintering: Phase evolution, microstructure and mechanical properties

context_update.summary: This chunk introduces the paper title, authors, abstract, and the beginning of the introduction. Key findings from the abstract include: after 35 h mechanical alloying, a Fe40Mn19Ni15Al15Si10C1 powder forms BCC (a=0.286 nm), τ-brass (a=0.872 nm), and partially ordered B2 (a=0.290 nm) phases with trace Si. The powder is thermally stable up to ~500 °C, with Fe5Si3 forming at ~520 °C. Spark plasma sintering (SPS) retains BCC and γ-brass/B2 phases while also forming Fe5Si3 silicide (hP16). The SPS sample shows low density (~6.49 g/cm³), high microhardness (~7.8 GPa), compressive strength (~2046 MPa), and ~19% ductility. Enhanced properties result from a dual-phase BCC+B2 microstructure with nano-silicides, leading to grain-boundary and dislocation strengthening. The introduction motivates low-density BCC+B2 high entropy steel design via MA and SPS. Subsequent chunks are expected to detail experimental procedures, phase evolution, microstructure characterization, thermal stability tests, and mechanical property data.
material_systems: Fe5Si3-type silicide (hP16, a=b=0.667 nm, c=0.468 nm)
microstructure: multiphase BCC + τ-brass + B2 after MA
retained BCC + γ-brass + B2 plus Fe5Si3 after SPS
dual-phase BCC+B2 with nano-silicide precipitates in SPS sample

context_update.summary: This chunk describes the mechanical testing protocols (microhardness with Oliver-Pharr, uniaxial compression at 25°C, 10^-3 s^-1) and begins the Results section. Melting point is calculated as 1638 K (ML) and 1620 K (rule of mixture), supported by Table 1 of binary liquidus/enthalpy. Phase evolution during mechanical alloying is detailed: Al diffuses fastest; after 25 h BCC+γ-brass solid solution forms; 35 h yields BCC+B2+γ-brass+ trace Si. Rietveld refinement (Table 2) quantifies crystallite size (10-20 nm), lattice strain (up to ~19000×10^-4), phase fractions, and dislocation density (up to 3.97×10^18 m^-2) across milling times. Powder morphology shows mean size 3-5 μm at 35 h, with EDS point analysis (Table 3) identifying Fe-rich BCC and Mn-rich γ-brass. TEM confirms nanocrystalline BCC rings. DTA and ex-situ XRD reveal thermal events at 420°C (γ-brass ordering) and 520°C (Fe5Si3 formation), stability up to 500°C. SPS sample (900°C,50MPa) contains BCC, B2, γ-brass, and Fe5Si3, with homogeneous element distribution per SEM/EDS and EPMA. Measured properties: microhardness 7.8 GPa, elastic modulus 184 GPa, compressive strength 2046 MPa, strain 19%. Subsequent chunks are expected to discuss strengthening mechanisms and property correlations.
processing_route: spark plasma sintering at 900 °C and 50 MPa
key_variables: melting point ML: 1638 K, rule of mixture: 1620 K

γ-brass type phase (Mn-rich)
B2-type ordered phase (Al,Ni-rich)
trace Si in milled powder; Fe5Si3 silicide appears after annealing above 500 °C and in SPSed sample
crystallite sizes 10-20 nm, increasing lattice strain and dislocation density with milling
polycrystalline BCC ring pattern in TEM

context_update.summary: This chunk reports mechanical properties of the SPSed Fe40Mn19Ni15Al15Si10C1 high entropy steel: indentation depth ~5.08 μm, no crack at 5000 mN load, microhardness 7.8±0.3 GPa, elastic modulus 184±5 GPa, compressive strength 2046±160 MPa, and strain 19% (Fig. 9). Comparative radar/plots vs. other alloys are given but not detailed numerically. The discussion of alloying behavior covers thermodynamic criteria (ΔSconf, ΔHmix, δ) from Table 6 (values not shown in chunk), and the role of VEC in dual-phase BCC+B2 formation. B2 ordering is attributed to highly negative Al–Fe and Al–Ni binary mixing enthalpies. Dissolution sequence during MA: C fastest, then Al, Mn, Ni; Si undissolved due to low diffusivity. BCC phase lattice parameter is close to α-Fe. Table 4 provides quantitative phase fractions, lattice parameters, and crystallite sizes for annealed samples (400, 500, 600, 800 °C) and the SPSed sample, showing Fe5Si3 appearance at 600 °C and its persistence at higher temperatures. Fig. 5 links Fe5Si3 formation to the 520 °C exotherm. Subsequent chunks will likely discuss thermal stability, strengthening mechanisms, and property correlations in more detail, and should retain the phase quantification and mechanical values provided here.

context_update.summary: Chunk discusses alloying behavior, thermal stability, and CALPHAD predictions for Fe40Mn19Ni15Al15Si10C1. During mechanical alloying, C dissolves first, then Al, Mn, Ni, while Si remains undissolved after 35 h due to low diffusivity. The BCC phase lattice parameter is near α-Fe. The alloy is thermally stable up to ~500 °C; at 420 °C BCC + γ-brass + B2 are present with increased γ-brass fraction, and at 520 °C Fe5Si3 silicide appears. Silicide formation is linked to undissolved Si and strong Fe–Si/Mn–Si negative enthalpies. BCC and B2 retention is ascribed to Fe–Al/Ni–Al bonding and sluggish diffusion. XRD (Fig. 6) confirms BCC + B2 + γ-brass + Fe5Si3 in the SPSed sample. SEM-EDS/WDS (Fig. 7, Table 5) reveals compositional differences between white (Fe-rich, Mn, Ni, low Al) and light grey (Ni, Al-rich) regions. Thermodynamic parameters (Table 6) yield ΔHmix=-8.4 kJ/mol, ΔSconf=12.4 J/K·mol, δ=7.6%, Ω=2.4, VEC=6.9. CALPHAD (Fig. 10, Table 8) predicts phase sequences; discrepancy with experiment is due to TCFE8 database. Subsequent chunks should remember the dissolution sequence, the thermal stability range, the dual-phase + silicide microstructure in SPSed state, and the compositional heterogeneity.
microstructure: SPSed sample: BCC + B2 + γ-brass type + Fe5Si3

rolling_context_summary:
Summary: Chunk discusses alloying behavior, thermal stability, and CALPHAD predictions for Fe40Mn19Ni15Al15Si10C1. During mechanical alloying, C dissolves first, then Al, Mn, Ni, while Si remains undissolved after 35 h due to low diffusivity. The BCC phase lattice parameter is near α-Fe. The alloy is thermally stable up to ~500 °C; at 420 °C BCC + γ-brass + B2 are present with increased γ-brass fraction, and at 520 °C Fe5Si3 silicide appears. Silicide formation is linked to undissolved Si and strong Fe–Si/Mn–Si negative enthalpies. BCC and B2 retention is ascribed to Fe–Al/Ni–Al bonding and sluggish diffusion. XRD (Fig. 6) confirms BCC + B2 + γ-brass + Fe5Si3 in the SPSed sample. SEM-EDS/WDS (Fig. 7, Table 5) reveals compositional differences between white (Fe-rich, Mn, Ni, low Al) and light grey (Ni, Al-rich) regions. Thermodynamic parameters (Table 6) yield ΔHmix=-8.4 kJ/mol, ΔSconf=12.4 J/K·mol, δ=7.6%, Ω=2.4, VEC=6.9. CALPHAD (Fig. 10, Table 8) predicts phase sequences; discrepancy with experiment is due to TCFE8 database. Subsequent chunks should remember the dissolution sequence, the thermal stability range, the dual-phase + silicide microstructure in SPSed state, and the compositional heterogeneity.
Material systems: Fe40Mn19Ni15Al15Si10C1 high entropy steel; BCC phase (cI2, a=0.286 nm); γ-brass type phase (cI52, a=0.872 nm); B2-type phase (cP2, a=0.290 nm); Fe5Si3-type silicide (hP16, a=b=0.667 nm, c=0.468 nm); BCC phase; γ-brass type phase; B2-type phase
Sample aliases: as-milled powder (35 h MA); SPSed sample; SPSed pellet; annealed powder at 400, 500, 600, 800 °C; non-equiatomic HES; as-milled powder (35 h)
Processing route: Mechanical alloying 35 h; Spark plasma sintering; mechanical alloying for 35 h; spark plasma sintering at 900 °C and 50 MPa; annealing at 400, 500, 600, 800 °C for phase stability; Annealing at 400, 500, 600, 800 °C; annealing at 420 °C, 520 °C
Key variables: milling time 35 h; thermal stability limit ~500 °C; phase transformation at ~520 °C; density ~6.49±0.3 g/cm³; microhardness ~7.8±0.3 GPa; compressive strength ~2046±160 MPa; ductility ~19%; milling time: 5, 15, 25, 30, 35 h
Microstructure: multiphase BCC + τ-brass + B2 after MA; retained BCC + γ-brass + B2 plus Fe5Si3 after SPS; dual-phase BCC+B2 with nano-silicide precipitates in SPS sample; BCC major phase with nanocrystalline grains (3-5 μm particles); γ-brass type phase (Mn-rich); B2-type ordered phase (Al,Ni-rich); trace Si in milled powder; Fe5Si3 silicide appears after annealing above 500 °C and in SPSed sample; crystallite sizes 10-20 nm, increasing lattice strain and dislocation density with milling
Properties: low density ~6.49 g/cm³; high microhardness ~7.8 GPa; compressive strength ~2046 MPa; ductility 19%; microhardness 7.8 ± 0.3 GPa; elastic modulus 184 ± 5 GPa; compressive ultimate strength 2046 ± 160 MPa; compressive strain ~19%

context_update.summary: This chunk closes the CALPHAD discrepancy note (TCFE8 vs TCHEA3) and covers the full Section 4.3 on mechanical properties and strengthening mechanisms of the SPSed Fe40Mn19Ni15Al15Si10C1. The measured compressive yield strength is 2046 ± 160 MPa, decomposed into frictional (120 MPa), Hall-Petch grain boundary (1085 MPa BCC, 1224 MPa B2), Bailey-Hirsch dislocation (687 MPa BCC, 590 MPa B2), Orowan precipitation (54+111 MPa BCC, 55 MPa B2), and Fleischer solid solution (106 MPa BCC, 177 MPa B2) contributions. Theoretical total is 2164 MPa, closely matching experiment. Dominant mechanisms are grain boundary (52.55%) and dislocation (30.00%). Microstructural inputs include BCC grain size 0.28 μm, B2 0.22 μm, Fe5Si3 vol. 8.4% radius 102 nm, B2 precipitates vol. 34% radius 220 nm, dislocation densities 4.46e15 and 3.36e15 m−2. Phase fractions were measured as BCC 56%, B2 34%, normalized to 62% and 38%. The alloy microhardness (7.8 GPa) far exceeds dual-phase steel (2.0–2.4 GPa); Fig. 9(e,f) and Table 9 / Supplementary Table S1 compare properties against HEAs and conventional steels. Ductility is ~19%. No Conclusion text beyond heading appeared. Future chunks should carry these strengthening contributions, phase/thickness parameters, and the cited figures/tables for any conclusion or extended discussion.
microstructure: BCC (56%, normalized 62%) with grain size 0.28 μm
B2 (34%, normalized 38%) with grain size 0.22 μm
Fe5Si3 precipitates (8.4 vol.%, radius 102 nm)
B2 precipitates (34 vol.%, radius 220 nm)
dislocation densities 4.46e15 m−2 (BCC), 3.36e15 m−2 (B2)
